title: Bipolar-doped superconducting infinite-layer cuprates
authors: Fengzhe Wang, Yueying Li, Heng Wang, Lizhi Xu, Xianfeng Wu, Lixiang Xu, Guangdi Zhou, Jin-Feng Jia, Peng Li, Haoliang Huang, Qi-Kun Xue, Zhuoyu Chen
abstract: Distilling the intrinsic physics of the superconducting CuO2 plane from the complexities of charge-reservoir layers is a defining challenge in high-temperature superconductivity. While superconducting electron-doped infinite-layer cuprates have been synthesized, controllable and uniform hole doping has long remained elusive despite exploratory attempts, limiting spectroscopic insights. Here, we realize bipolar doping across infinite-layer (Sr,Eu)CuO2 and (Ca,Li)CuO2+δ single-crystalline thin films, mapping the electronic phase diagram. Both electron- and hole-doped films show pronounced electrical resistance anisotropy, indicating the quasi-two-dimensional nature of the CuO2 planes. Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy across electron- and hole-doped regimes reveals persistent antiferromagnetic band folding coexisting with superconductivity. Remarkably, at a hole doping ~0.07 determined by Luttinger volume, the antiferromagnetic folding emerges from Fermi arcs within the film's single Fermi surface, with the onset superconducting transition temperature exceeding 60 K. These findings redefine the interplay between magnetic order and superconductivity and establish a definitive platform to investigate the intrinsic mechanism of high-temperature superconducting cuprates.
summary: This work reports on the synthesis and characterization of bipolar-doped infinite-layer cuprates, providing an unprecedented platform to study high-temperature superconductivity. By combining ARPES and transport measurements, the authors demonstrate the intrinsic coexistence of antiferromagnetic order and superconductivity in both electron- and hole-doped regimes. This research significantly advances the understanding of the magnetic mechanisms underlying cuprate superconductivity.
main_problem: The primary challenge is isolating the intrinsic superconducting physics of the $	ext{CuO}_2$ plane from the complexities introduced by charge-reservoir layers in high-temperature cuprate superconductors.
main_result: The authors successfully realized bipolar doping in infinite-layer cuprates, demonstrating that antiferromagnetic band folding coexists with high-temperature superconductivity ($T_{c	ext{onset}} > 60 	ext{ K}$) at a specific hole doping level ($p=0.07$).
method: The study employed a combination of advanced experimental techniques, including Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES), transport measurements (R-T curves), and Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM) on single-crystalline thin films.
model_or_system: The research focuses on infinite-layer cuprates, specifically electron-doped $(	ext{Sr},	ext{Eu})	ext{CuO}_2$ and hole-doped $(	ext{Ca},	ext{Li})	ext{CuO}_{2+\delta}$ single-crystalline thin films, which serve as a platform to study the interplay between magnetism and superconductivity.
key_observables: Electrical resistance anisotropy, superconducting transition temperature ($T_{c	ext{onset}}$), Fermi surface topology (single surface), and the presence/nature of antiferromagnetic (AFM) band folding.
important_parameters: Hole doping level ($p=0.07$), electron doping level ($	ext{Eu} \approx 0.11$), and the superconducting critical temperature ($T_c$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed phenomena are intrinsic to the $	ext{CuO}_2$ plane, and the superconducting response can be modeled using 2D Ginzburg-Landau theory.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the bipolar-doped phase diagram via R-T curves, demonstrate strong 2D anisotropy in superconductivity, and use ARPES to map the Fermi surface showing coexisting AFM folding and superconductivity.
paper_structure: The paper systematically presents the realization of bipolar doping, characterizes the electronic structure using ARPES to map the phase diagram, and uses transport/structural measurements to confirm the quasi-2D nature and the coexistence of magnetic order and superconductivity.
why_interesting: The definitive platform established by this work—allowing the study of both electron and hole doping regimes on the same material type—provides crucial, clean data for theorists attempting to build a unified theory of high-Tc superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
